extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a resistive heat‑bed, an internal fan to homogenise air temperature, two embedded temperature sensors monitoring the internal air and one external sensor measuring ambient temperature. The plant is actuated and monitored by a Raspberry Pi running a low‑level driver that reads the sensors and issues commands to the heat‑bed and fan via a RabbitMQ communication server. Its function is to achieve and maintain a user‑defined set‑point temperature inside the box regardless of the payload, making it a representative cyber‑physical system for digital‑twin research.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides (1) real‑time visualization of the incubator status by ingesting controller and driver messages into a time‑series database and rendering them on a dashboard; (2) on‑demand calibration of the thermal models using automated calibration routines; (3) anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation implemented with a Kalman filter that fuses measured temperatures with Model B predictions; (4) what‑if simulations via co‑simulation that replay historical or live PT data to evaluate alternative controller parameters; and (5) a self‑adaptation loop that detects plant behavior changes, schedules and executes data‑gathering experiments, re‑calibrates the model, re‑configures the Kalman filter and updates the controller settings accordingly.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B as the cyber‑physical controller, the RabbitMQ message broker for synchronization and message passing, a low‑level driver that abstracts sensor/actuator communication and enforces a safe protocol, and a bang‑bang controller with configurable lower/upper limits, heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C). Calibration algorithms adjust model parameters, a Kalman filter algorithm provides state estimation for anomaly detection, and a co‑simulation capability supports what‑if analysis. All hardware schematics, software source code, and datasets are published openly in the GitHub repository https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Synchronization between the Physical Twin and Digital Twin components is realised through a RabbitMQ message broker. The low‑level driver periodically reads temperature sensors and publishes the measurements to RabbitMQ; the controller subscribes to these measurement messages and publishes command messages for the fan and heat‑bed. DT services such as the real‑time visualisation dashboard, Kalman‑filter based anomaly detector, and on‑demand calibration consume the same streams, enabling near‑real‑time operation. The documents do not provide explicit sampling rates, latency bounds, or temporal granularity, only that the driver performs periodic reads.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT is orchestrated as a component‑based system where data, models and algorithms are separate services coordinated by a central RabbitMQ communication server. Sensors feed data to a low‑level driver, which forwards measurements to RabbitMQ; the controller receives the data, computes control actions and publishes its state. Calibration algorithms subscribe to the same streams to update model parameters, while monitors subscribe to detect safety or performance violations. Humans or higher‑level algorithms can trigger re‑configuration. This matches the generic CPS‑based Digital Twin pattern (Fig. 1) with a centralized message broker mediating all interactions.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external components is achieved through the RabbitMQ message broker, which enables publish/subscribe exchange of sensor data and controller commands. The DT can forward these streams to a time‑series database and a dashboard for real‑time visualisation, and can supply historical or live data to co‑simulation modules for what‑if simulations, as outlined in the digital‑twinning goals. No further external systems or interoperability standards are specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Two analytical behavioral models of the incubator temperature dynamics are defined: Model A, based on all four modelling assumptions, and Model B, which relaxes Assumption 4 (heatbed does not absorb heat). Both models derive from the heat balance equation Q = c m ΔT and use a small number of free parameters (Model A with two free parameters, Model B with four free parameters). Temperature data are generated by the three temperature sensors (two interior, one exterior) and are collected by the low‑level driver, which publishes the measurements as messages to the RabbitMQ communication server. Calibration algorithms consume these sensor data to estimate the model parameters and to update the digital twin’s model instance; the calibrated Model B has been shown experimentally to better predict the thermal inertia of the heatbed.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the incubator is performed by a resistive heat‑bed (on/off heating element) and a small fan (on/off) mounted inside the insulated styrofoam box. Both actuators are driven by a Raspberry Pi‑based controller that implements a bang‑bang control strategy with configurable lower‑limit (LL) and upper‑limit (UL) temperature thresholds, a heating duration (H) and a waiting duration (C). Actuator commands are encoded as messages sent to the RabbitMQ server; the low‑level driver receives these messages and issues the corresponding electrical signals to the heat‑bed and fan. Safety constraints are enforced by a safe‑protocol embedded in the low‑level driver, which ensures the device remains in a safe state and imposes a waiting period after each actuation to avoid temperature overshoot.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing subsystem comprises three temperature sensors: two mounted inside the styrofoam box to monitor the internal air temperature and one mounted outside to measure ambient temperature. The low‑level driver periodically reads the sensor outputs, packages the measurements into messages, and publishes them to the RabbitMQ server. These measurements constitute the primary data source for the digital twin’s monitoring and calibration processes.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The fidelity of the digital twin models is governed by four explicit modelling assumptions: (1) uniform temperature distribution inside the box, ensured by the internal fan; (2) negligible heat accumulation in the box walls; (3) constant specific heat capacity of the air; and (4) nearly all electrical power is converted to heat transferred directly to the air (Model A) – Model B relaxes assumption 4. Model validity is established experimentally by calibrating the free parameters against the sensor data; calibration experiments showed that Model B provides a more accurate prediction of the heat‑bed’s thermal inertia. Calibration algorithms update the model parameters using the collected temperature measurements, thereby maintaining consistency between the physical twin and its digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data are acquired by the low‑level driver that periodically reads the three temperature sensors (two inside the incubator, one outside). The driver packages the raw temperature values and the current PT state and publishes them as messages to the RabbitMQ communication server. DT components subscribe to these messages to obtain real‑time sensor readings. In the experimental setup the two interior sensor values are averaged to form a single box‑air temperature before being used by the DT.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Digital Twin components send control commands (e.g., turn fan on/off, start heating, set heating duration H, waiting duration C, adjust lower/upper temperature limits LL/UL) as RabbitMQ messages to the controller. The controller, implemented as a bang‑bang style controller, receives these messages from the RabbitMQ server, translates them into actuator commands, and forwards them to the low‑level driver, which applies them to the physical actuators (fan and heat‑bed). The low‑level driver incorporates a safe protocol that validates commands before execution and reports the resulting control state back to RabbitMQ, providing execution confirmation and feedback to the DT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All software components (low‑level driver, controller, DT modules) communicate via a centrally deployed RabbitMQ message broker. RabbitMQ provides the synchronization and message‑passing backbone; each component publishes to or subscribes from the broker. The deployment is described as lightweight and easy to install on‑premise or in the cloud, suitable for large numbers of users. The network thus consists of a Raspberry Pi hosting the controller and driver connected to the RabbitMQ server over the local network.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering of the incubator Digital Twin follows an iterative modelling‑calibration workflow. Temperature data from two internal sensors and one external sensor are collected by a low‑level driver, uploaded to a RabbitMQ message broker, and consumed by a controller running on a Raspberry Pi. Two simulation models (Model A and Model B) of the thermal dynamics are constructed; calibration algorithms use the streamed data to estimate the models' free parameters. Experimental runs described in the report provide data for validating and refining the models. All hardware and software specifications are maintained in a publicly available open‑source repository.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The incubator Digital Twin delivers decision support through several capabilities: (1) Real‑time visualization of the incubator status on a dashboard that consumes time‑series data streamed from the low‑level driver via RabbitMQ; (2) On‑demand calibration that automatically re‑estimates model parameters when fresh temperature data are collected; (3) Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation performed by a Kalman filter that fuses Model B predictions with measured temperatures to infer the unseen heat‑bed temperature and flag deviations; (4) What‑if simulations carried out by co‑simulating the calibrated models with historical or live PT data, enabling exploration of alternative controller settings; and (5) A self‑adaptation loop that, upon detecting a behavioral change (e.g., insertion of a cold object), orchestrates data‑collection experiments, re‑calibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, runs optimisation simulations, and finally re‑configures the controller. These analytics provide actionable insights for maintaining target temperature, improving energy efficiency, and ensuring safe operation.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The DT incorporates safety mechanisms through a low‑level driver that embeds a safe protocol to keep the physical incubator in a safe state. Communication among the controller, low‑level driver, and the DT is performed via a RabbitMQ message broker, which provides reliable, lightweight messaging deployed on‑premises or in the cloud. The documents do not describe additional cybersecurity measures such as authentication, encryption, access‑control, or threat mitigation; thus, security considerations are limited to the safe protocol in the driver and the use of RabbitMQ for dependable data exchange.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular

We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed

**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided

detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
